item.title: Venice, the Doge's Palace
item.lesson: Renoir reached Venice in the autumn of 1881, a Parisian on his first Italian tour, and did the most natural thing in the world: he floated out on the water and painted the postcard view — the Doge's Palace, pink and white across the basin — in a shower of confetti strokes. Where Whistler's Venice was a held breath and Manet's was a cool appraisal, Renoir's is pure appetite. The palace's Gothic arcades become a lace of sherbet color; the lagoon is stitched from a hundred short dashes of green, violet, and cream; even the shadows look pleased. He was almost apologetic about the joy — he wrote home that he was doing Venice 'like everyone else,' and within weeks he stood before Raphael in Rome and began doubting Impressionism entirely, tightening his style for years afterward. That makes this canvas a kind of farewell performance: the loose, sun-drunk brush at its last uncomplicated moment, spending itself on water and light without a second thought.
item.lookFor: Squint until the palace almost disappears — the picture still works, because the color intervals alone carry the light. That is the Impressionist wager in its purest form.
